---
title: "Derivation 3.6A — Stationary correspondence calibration"
---

# Derivation 3.6A — Stationary correspondence calibration {#sec-derivation-03-06A-stationary-correspondence}

## Purpose

This appendix supports Chapter 3.6. It collects the calibration steps that connect the native M1 stationary source-to-field map to familiar stationary gravitational variables.

The appendix is deliberately downstream of the M1 construction. It does not define the primitive source grammar. It checks how that grammar faces ordinary density, current, Newtonian potential, and metric-facing language.

## Role / regime / status

- **Role:** correspondence and calibration.
- **Regime:** stationary weak-to-moderate overlap; static isotropic scalar limit and transverse shift comparison.
- **Status:** stable comparison sheet for Part III.
- **Non-claim:** successful correspondence is not a full native derivation of all GR dynamics.

## Source-density calibration

The native even source package has units

$$
[M_k]=\mathrm{kg\,s^{-1}m^{-2}}.
$$

Ordinary mass density has units

$$
[\rho]=\mathrm{kg\,m^{-3}}.
$$

The extra factor is one power of velocity. In the static isotropic scalar correspondence, the even M1 packages therefore satisfy

$$
\sum_k M_k \longleftrightarrow c\rho.
$$

For an isotropic static source, a convenient axis-wise representation is

$$
M_x=M_y=M_z=\frac{c\rho}{3},
$$

or, when the directional imbalance vanishes,

$$
J_k^+=J_k^-=\frac{c\rho}{3},
\qquad
P_k=0.
$$

The six positive channels are not six copies of the classical source. They are a directional-positive representation whose even packages compress to the scalar classical source in the static overlap.

## Newtonian scalar limit

The M1 scalar equation is

$$
\Delta\theta_0=-\frac{4\pi G_N}{c^3}\sum_kM_k.
$$

Using the static isotropic calibration gives

$$
\Delta\theta_0=-\frac{4\pi G_N}{c^2}\rho.
$$

Define the ordinary Newtonian potential by

$$
U:=-c^2\theta_0.
$$

Then

$$
\Delta U=4\pi G_N\rho.
$$

For an exterior spherical source,

$$
U(r)=-\frac{G_NM}{r},
$$

so

$$
\theta_0(r)=\frac{G_NM}{c^2r},
\qquad
G(r)=\sqrt{1-\frac{2G_NM}{c^2r}}.
$$

This checks that the additive/even source package produces the usual scalar weak-field face.

## Shift-current comparison

The directional M1 field is built from the odd package $P_k$. In a stationary gauge-fixed comparison, the familiar object is a transverse current-like source $j_i^\perp$.

The comparison equation is

$$
\Delta A_i=\frac{16\pi G_N}{c^3}j_i^\perp,
\qquad
\partial_iA_i=0.
$$

This is a calibration statement, not a replacement of the native source grammar. It says how the M1 shift sector is recognized in the weak stationary current language.

## Metric-facing dictionary

At weak-field stationary order, the metric-facing dictionary can be written schematically as

$$
g_{00}=-(1+2U/c^2),
$$

and

$$
g_{0i}\sim\frac{A_i}{c}.
$$

The scalar package supplies the clock-depth or lapse-like side. The directional package supplies the mixed time-space, frame-drag or shift-like side. Signs and coefficients in the second relation depend on comparison convention; the structural split is the robust point.

## Result

The stationary correspondence layer establishes the classical faces

$$
\sum_kM_k\leftrightarrow c\rho,
\qquad
\theta_0\leftrightarrow U,
\qquad
A_i\leftrightarrow\text{stationary shift}.
$$

It leaves the order of explanation intact: first the native M1 source-to-field map, then the classical/GR-facing calibration.
